coredata.dc:title: Extended Long Short-Term Memory network for robust state-of-health estimation of lithium-ion batteries under diverse charging strategies
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Electrical Power & Energy Systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Xiangjian
Xu, Shixin
Yu, Yongjin
Zhu, Yanzheng
Gao, Feng
coredata.dc:description:
Accurate assessment of the health status of lithium-ion batteries is crucial for ensuring the safety and efficiency of their industrial applications. Various methods have been proposed to estimate the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries, but most of these methods are only applicable to specific types of batteries or operating conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposed an Extended Long Short-Term Memory (xLSTM) network for SOH estimation under various battery charging strategies. In this study, features are derived from the incremental capacity (IC) curve of the discharge process, which serve as primary indicators of battery health. To improve the accuracy of estimations, voltage and current features from both the charging and discharging phases are integrated as supplementary characteristics. The Spearman correlation coefficient is utilized to identify and select features that exhibit high correlation, thereby excluding irrelevant parameters. The proposed xLSTM model integrates the architectures of sLSTM and mLSTM, facilitating the effective capture of intricate temporal dependencies and nonlinear relationships associated with battery degradation. Experimental results demonstrate significant performance improvements, achieving average MAPE of 0.20% and R
2
of 0.997, outperforming existing methods by over 40% in accuracy. Cross-chemistry validation on both LFP and NCM batteries confirms the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
State of health
Extended Long Short-Term Memory
Spearman correlation
Incremental capacity curve

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feature selection based on Spearman correlation coefficient
SOH estimation performance for LFP batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comprehensive framework for Lithium-ion battery health assessment using xLSTM.

(a) IC curve of battery No. 5, (b) IC curve of battery No. 55, (c) IC curve of battery No. 85, (d) Current versus time curve of battery No. 5, (e) Current versus time curve of battery No. 55, (f) Current versus time curve of battery No. 85, (g) Voltage versus time curve of battery No. 5, (h) Voltage versus time curve of battery No. 55, (i) Voltage versus time curve of battery No. 85.
Average spearman correlation matrix with SOH.
Long Short-Term Memory network cell structure diagram.
xLSTM network architecture for SOH estimation.

Prediction results for the MIT battery test dataset. (a), (b) and (c) show the full cycle prediction and true values, scatter plot of predicted versus actual values, and prediction error for each cycle for batteries b1c15, b1c27, b1c56, b1c69, b1c84, and b1c97, respectively.
NCM#6 Battery Spearman Correlation Coefficient Heatmap.
Comparison of metrics across different layers of xLSTM.
Testing battery data for MIT LFP batteries.
Performance comparison demonstrating xLSTM architectural advantages on LFP battery datasets.
SOH correlation coefficients comparison between Kendall and Pearson methods.
Performance comparison using different feature selection strategies.
Performance comparison using only IC curve features.
Performance comparison of various models on NCM battery datasets.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This document is the results of the research project funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [62403288].

head.ce:title: Extended Long Short-Term Memory network for robust state-of-health estimation of lithium-ion batteries under diverse charging strategies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=College of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Shandong University of Science and Technology, city=Qingdao, country=China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Control Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Control Science and Engineering, Shandong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=School of Control Science and Engineering, Shandong University, city=Jinan, country=China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Accurate assessment of the health status of lithium-ion batteries is crucial for ensuring the safety and efficiency of their industrial applications. Various methods have been proposed to estimate the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries, but most of these methods are only applicable to specific types of batteries or operating conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposed an Extended Long Short-Term Memory (xLSTM) network for SOH estimation under various battery charging strategies. In this study, features are derived from the incremental capacity (IC) curve of the discharge process, which serve as primary indicators of battery health. To improve the accuracy of estimations, voltage and current features from both the charging and discharging phases are integrated as supplementary characteristics. The Spearman correlation coefficient is utilized to identify and select features that exhibit high correlation, thereby excluding irrelevant parameters. The proposed xLSTM model integrates the architectures of sLSTM and mLSTM, facilitating the effective capture of intricate temporal dependencies and nonlinear relationships associated with battery degradation. Experimental results demonstrate significant performance improvements, achieving average MAPE of 0.20% and R
2
of 0.997, outperforming existing methods by over 40% in accuracy. Cross-chemistry validation on both LFP and NCM batteries confirms the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have become the preferred energy solution for electric vehicles, mobile devices, and grid energy storage systems due to their high energy density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rate [1]. With advancements in materials and manufacturing technologies, lithium-ion batteries have assumed an increasingly significant role in modern society [2]. However, over time, the capacity and performance of lithium-ion batteries will inevitably deteriorate, which will significantly affect the reliability and safety of the associated devices, Therefore, accurate estimation of the State-of-Health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is crucial not only for optimizing Battery Management Systems (BMS) [3], but also for extending battery life, ensuring safe operation of devices, and reducing maintenance costs [4].

The decline in lithium-ion battery performance is primarily manifested as capacity fade and increased internal resistance due to the irreversible side reactions occurring inside the battery [5]. It has been proven that the main side reactions include the growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), particle cracking, and lithium plating [6]. These side reactions lead to the continuous loss of lithium-ion inventory (LLI) and active material (LAM) [7]. Notably, these reactions are also interdependent, making their quantification extremely challenging. Accurately assessing the SOH of lithium-ion batteries remains a challenging task. To achieve precise evaluations, numerous researchers have conducted extensive studies. Traditional SOH estimation methods can be categorized into empirical approaches and model-based approaches [8,9]. Empirical approaches use mathematical formulas that represent capacity degradation and its relationship with the operational conditions of lithium-ion batteries, including linear, exponential, and Arrhenius. Influencing factors encompass depth of discharge (DOD), temperature, current rate (C-rate), storage time, and Coulombic efficiency [10,11]. Model-based approaches rely on constructing accurate equivalent battery models to simulate battery behavior. These models include electrochemical models (EM) and equivalent circuit models (ECM) [12,13]. By integrating algorithms such as the Kalman filter family and the particle filter family, these approaches iteratively calculate battery SOH [14,15]. However, both model-based methods and empirical face significant limitations in practical applications due to the dynamic and complex operating conditions of lithium-ion batteries, including varying temperature, charging rates, and usage patterns. These real-world variables make it challenging to accurately capture all the factors affecting battery degradation.

With the development of artificial intelligence algorithm, data-driven approaches have been widely applied for the lifetime prediction of lithium-ion batteries. Various machine learning methods, such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) [16], and Support Vector Regression (SVR) [17], have been employed for state-of-health estimation. However, due to the complex nonlinear degradation process of batteries, the application of simple machine learning (ML) models is limited. Deep learning (DL), as a ML technique that has demonstrated superior performance in various fields, has been successfully used for SOH estimation. DL models can be categorized into two types: sequence-to-sequence and feature engineering-based methods. The sequence-to-sequence models leverage Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to automatically extract features from raw charge–discharge data [18–21]. In specific, Ref. [18] proposed a correlation-augmented Informer-based method incorporating CNN for automatic feature extraction, which achieves real-time state-of-health estimation of Li-ion batteries through multiple correlation coefficient analysis and an improved attention mechanism. Ref. [19] proposed a domain adaptation-based method for lithium-ion battery state-of-health estimation, which combines CNN for automatic feature extraction and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM, realizing SOH estimation across different battery chemistries and operating conditions through feature extraction from partial charging data and adversarial training. Ref. [20] proposed a transfer learning-based method for personalized SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries, which used CNN for automatic feature extraction from raw charging voltage data with an improved maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) domain adaptation technique and achieved SOH estimation across different battery types and operating conditions. Ref. [21] proposed a hybrid neural network model named Multi-scale Convolutional Attention (MCA) mechanism for battery SOH estimation, which integrates CNN for feature extraction, a novel MCA block with channel and spatial attention modules to automatically capture degradation features, and a residual module to improve estimation stability. The proposed MCA mechanism demonstrates superior ability in dynamically adjusting attention weights based on training samples to avoid overfitting issues. However, those methods need to process large scale of data, which will significantly increase the computation burden. Feature engineering-based methods exhibit high performance and depend significantly on the quality of extracted features. In specific, Ref. [22] proposed a lithium-ion battery state-of-health estimation method based on health indicator extraction and bidirectional long short-term memory networks, which extracted health indicators from charge–discharge curves through battery aging mechanism analysis and used BiLSTM to capture bidirectional time series dependencies, realizing accurate SOH estimation. Ref. [23] proposed a comparative study of multiple models including CNN, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), Gate Recurrent Unit (GRU) and attention, which could extract and combine features from different perspectives. The prediction performance of different features and model combinations were experimentally compared. Ref. [24] proposed a BiLSTM-Transformer model for lithium-ion battery SOH estimation during fast charging, which extracted four health-correlated features (charging time, average voltage, charging capacity, voltage curve area) from constant current charging stage. The model combines bidirectional LSTM for capturing long-term dependencies and transformer’s multi-head attention mechanism for enhanced feature learning, achieving estimation under various fast charging rates (1.5C-5.0C).

Most existing ML methods for battery SOH estimation do not consider how to effectively extract common features from batteries operating under different charging strategies. Therefore, this paper proposes a novel approach that combines the Extended Long Short-Term Memory (xLSTM) network with Spearman’s rank correlation analysis. Unlike existing methods, this work incorporates features from the incremental capacity (IC) curve, which significantly improves the SOH estimation accuracy.

Various deep learning models, including CNNs, traditional LSTMs, and Transformers, have been utilized for SOH estimation, but each has its own drawbacks. CNNs are good at automatic feature extraction but have difficulty with temporal dependencies. Standard LSTMs encounter issues with information loss when dealing with long-term dependencies, while Transformers demand significant computational resources and large datasets for training. Given the complex nonlinear nature and long-term temporal dependencies involved in battery degradation, this paper suggests using an xLSTM architecture. This model integrates the normalizer state mechanism of sLSTM with the matrix memory capabilities of mLSTM, allowing for a more effective understanding of battery degradation under different charging strategies.

In contrast to current methodologies that predominantly emphasize either sophisticated neural network architectures or traditional voltage–current characteristics, this study introduces an innovative approach that effectively combines xLSTM architectural advancements with feature extraction based on incremental capacity (IC) curves. The deliberate focus on IC curve features, which have been substantiated through selection based on Spearman correlation, tackles the significant challenge of accurately capturing essential electrochemical degradation mechanisms across various charging strategies. This physics-informed, multi-source feature fusion technique guarantees strong performance in SOH estimation while preserving the ability to generalize across different battery chemistries, ranging from LFP to NCM batteries. The selection of xLSTM architecture combined with Spearman correlation-based feature selection is strategically motivated. The hybrid memory mechanisms of xLSTM provide superior capability in capturing long-term dependencies and complex nonlinear patterns inherent in battery degradation processes, while Spearman correlation ensures robust feature selection against non-linear relationships and measurement noise, creating a synergistic framework that addresses both temporal complexity and feature reliability challenges in SOH estimation.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 presents the principle of proposed methodology, including the data processing techniques, feature extraction and selection methods, and the structure of the xLSTM model. Section 3 provides a case study along with a comprehensive comparison of the proposed methodology against other leading contemporary approaches. Section 4 involves the conclusions.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the proposed SOH estimation methodology for lithium-ion batteries is structured into three steps. In the first step, the MIT battery dataset is analyzed and processed. Various charging strategies are applied to investigate the battery SOH variations through IC curves, current curves, and voltage curves, in relation to cycle changes. Common features that are closely associated with SOH degradation are extracted, capturing characteristics shared by the majority of the batteries. To further enhance prediction accuracy, the extracted features are filtered using Spearman correlation coefficients, allowing the selection of highly correlated features as health indicators, which are used as inputs for the model. The processed battery data is then divided into training and testing datasets. The second step involves constructing an enhanced LSTM model. Specifically, the proposed model combined sLSTM and mLSTM to form a three-layer xLSTM architecture [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’], combined with fully connected layers. The training dataset is used to train the model, ultimately resulting in a predictive model capable of directly estimating battery SOH. In the third step, the trained model is evaluated using the testing dataset, which consists of previously unused battery data. The accuracy of the SOH prediction is validated by calculating metrics.

The State-of-Health (SOH) serves as the fundamental metric for quantifying battery degradation. In this study, SOH is calculated using the standard capacity-based definition:
(1)
SOH =
Q
k
Q
n
× 100 %
where
Q
k
is the maximum discharge capacity at the current cycle k , and
Q
n
is the initial maximum discharge capacity at the beginning of life.

In the datasets used in this study, all discharge capacity measurements were obtained under standardized laboratory conditions, ensuring consistent and reliable SOH reference values with minimal measurement uncertainty. This controlled experimental environment significantly contributes to the high precision achievable in our estimation model.

A significant advancement in this research lies in the strategic integration of features derived from multiple sources, with a particular focus on the characteristics of incremental capacity (IC) curves. In contrast to existing methodologies that predominantly depend on conventional voltage-current features, this approach acknowledges the essential role of IC curves in illustrating the internal degradation mechanisms of batteries. These curves effectively capture nuanced electrochemical changes that transpire during aging processes, thereby offering more profound insights into capacity fade mechanisms than traditional metrics alone. By amalgamating these IC derived features with meticulously selected voltage and current characteristics, this method establishes a comprehensive feature set that sustains high prediction accuracy across various charging strategies. This multi-source feature fusion strategy significantly enhances the model’s adaptability to diverse operational conditions while yielding more robust SOH estimations, thereby addressing a critical limitation present in current battery health monitoring techniques.

This study, which differs from existing research often focusing on specific battery strategies, concentrates on extracting features from relevant stages that are common to batteries subjected to diverse operational strategies. The IC curve, which effectively reflects the internal degradation process of batteries, serves as a primary source of these features. As the battery’s state-of-health deteriorates, certain characteristics of the IC curve undergo significant changes, a phenomenon observed across batteries under various strategies. Consequently, extracting features from the IC curve can substantially enhance the model’s accuracy in predicting the SOH for batteries subjected to different strategies. To further improve prediction stability and mitigate the impact of noise inherent in features derived from the IC curve, curve features are also extracted from current and voltage curves. This comprehensive approach ensures a more robust and reliable assessment of battery health across diverse operational conditions. By integrating features from multiple sources, the proposed method aims to provide a more holistic and accurate battery health prediction, applicable to a wide range of battery management strategies.

In Fig. 2(d), (e), and (f), the significant changes in the constant current charging time at 1C after reaching 80% SOC and the constant current discharging time at 4C are evident. These time duration are denoted as health features
S
11
and
S
12
respectively. In Fig. 2(g), (h), and (i), the constant voltage charging time after 80% SOC also shows significant changes, denoted as
S
13
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feature selection based on Spearman correlation coefficient
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic combination of xLSTM and Spearman correlation-based feature selection offers distinct advantages for battery SOH estimation. Unlike Pearson correlation which assumes linear relationships, Spearman’s rank correlation is non-parametric and captures monotonic relationships without distributional assumptions, making it particularly suitable for battery degradation data which often exhibits non-linear patterns and outliers from measurement noise. This robustness is crucial when dealing with IC curve features that may have complex, non-linear correlations with SOH. Furthermore, the xLSTM architecture’s enhanced memory mechanisms effectively leverage these carefully selected features, as the reduced feature dimensionality allows the model to focus computational resources on learning intricate temporal dependencies rather than filtering noise from irrelevant parameters. This combination ensures both statistical robustness in feature selection and superior temporal modeling capability, addressing the dual challenges of feature relevance and complex degradation dynamics in battery systems.

The Spearman correlation coefficient is a non-parametric measure of rank correlation. It assesses the degree of correlation between two variables. The formula for the Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient is given by [25]:
(2)
ρ = 1 −
6 ∑
d
i
2
n
(
n
2
− 1 )
where
d
i
is the difference between the ranks of each observation, n is the number of observations. The correlation coefficient ranges from −1 to 1, where 1 indicates a perfect positive correlation, −1 indicates a perfect negative correlation, and 0 indicates no correlation. In correlation analysis, a Spearman rank correlation coefficient with an absolute value greater than 0.8 is generally considered to indicate a very strong correlation. For the extracted health factors mentioned above, an analysis was conducted using data from all batteries across three batches. The analyzed data was used to generate a heatmap, as shown in Fig. 3.

From the heatmap, it is evident that the IC curve slope features
S
1
(tangential value at 3.23 V),
S
2
(tangential value at 3.18 V), and the minimum value
S
7
, along with the corresponding voltage
S
8
and the area enclosed by the IC curve
S
10
(area between the curve and the line dQ/dV = −1), have correlation coefficients greater than 0.8. Similarly, the current features
S
11
(1 A constant current charging time after reaching 80% SOC) and
S
12
(4 A constant current discharging time), representing the 1 A constant current charging time and 4 A constant current discharging time, respectively, also exhibit correlation coefficients exceeding 0.8. The feature selection methodology presented in this work represents a significant innovation through its synergistic integration of electrochemical domain knowledge with data-driven statistical analysis. Unlike purely statistical approaches that may select mathematically relevant but physically meaningless features, or purely theoretical methods that might overlook statistical significance, our approach ensures selected features are both statistically robust and physically interpretable. Each selected feature corresponds to specific electrochemical processes in battery degradation: the IC curve slopes (
S
1
,
S
2
) reflect phase transition kinetics, the minimum values (
S
7
,
S
8
) indicate active material availability, and the IC curve area (
S
10
) quantifies total capacity during main phase transitions. This physics-informed feature selection enhances model interpretability, generalization capability across different batteries, and provides deeper insights into the underlying degradation mechanisms. The significant performance improvements demonstrated in our results validate the effectiveness of this novel approach to feature selection in battery SOH estimation. Based on the Spearman correlation coefficient screening, these seven features were selected as health factors and input into the model for SOH assessment.

While LSTM and IC curve analysis are recognized methodologies in battery research, the novelty of this study lies in their systematic integration within an advanced xLSTM framework. The xLSTM comprises two key components: sLSTM (scalar Long Short-Term Memory) and mLSTM (matrix Long Short-Term Memory). The sLSTM extends traditional LSTM with a scalar normalizer state that stabilizes exponential gates and prevents gradient explosion, while mLSTM employs a matrix memory structure that enhances capacity for capturing complex temporal patterns. By strategically combining these components with physics-informed feature extraction from IC curves and voltage-current characteristics, and employing Spearman correlation for robust feature selection, the proposed methodology achieves both superior statistical performance and physical interpretability tailored to battery degradation dynamics.

LSTM networks, a special type of RNN, effectively address the vanishing and exploding gradient problems inherent in traditional RNNs [26]. The basic structure of LSTM is shown in Fig. 4. LSTM networks, through their unique gating mechanisms, can effectively handle and remember dependencies in long time series data, making them widely used for time series prediction. However, considering that the degradation of battery health status is a complex nonlinear process, the traditional LSTM network structure struggles to accurately predict changes in battery SOH. Therefore, this paper adopts the xLSTM model [27], which is a novel hybrid architecture that combines the strengths of both sLSTM and mLSTM in a flexible manner. The sLSTM modifies the LSTM architecture by introducing an additional normalizer state that sums up the product of the input gate and all future forget gates. The forward pass of sLSTM is given by the following equations:
(3)

t
=
f

+

t
(5)
h

=
o
t

c
t
n
t

c
t
is the cell state,
n
t
is the normalizer state, and
h
t
is the hidden state. The input gate
i
t
, forget gate
f
t
, and output gate
o
t

To prevent potential overflows caused by the exponential functions, we stabilize the gates using an additional stabilizer state
m

in the forward pass, the sLSTM maintains stability without changing the output or the loss gradients with respect to the parameters.

mLSTM represents a significant advancement over traditional LSTM architectures for industrial applications. Its enhanced memory capacity, improved information retrieval mechanisms, and computational efficiency make it particularly suitable for complex industrial processes where accurate temporal pattern recognition and efficient processing are crucial.

The mLSTM introduces a matrix memory
C
t
∈
R
d × d
, where d is the dimension of the key and value vectors. At each time step t , mLSTM stores a pair of key
k
t
∈
R
d
and value
v
t
∈
R
d
vectors. The update and retrieval processes are defined as follows:
(12)

To stabilize the exponential gates and prevent overflow, we use the same stabilization technique as in sLSTM, involving the stabilizer state

In the xLSTM architecture, layers can be composed of either sLSTM or mLSTM blocks, specified by a configuration string. For example, a configuration of [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’, ‘m’] would create a 4-layer xLSTM with sLSTM blocks in the first and third layers, and mLSTM blocks in the second and fourth layers. This flexibility allows to experiment with various combinations to find optimal configurations for specific tasks. Therefore, the xLSTM can be defined as follows:
(24)
xLSTM =
{
L
1
,
L
2
, … ,
L
n
}
,
L
i
∈
{ s,mLSTM }
where
L
i
represents the i th layer, which can be either an sLSTM or mLSTM block.

The forward pass through the xLSTM processes input sequences by applying each layer successively:
(25)
h
t
i
,
c
t
i
=
L
i
(
h
t
i − 1
,
c
t
i − 1
)
,
i = 1 , … , n
where
h
t
i
and
c
t
i
are the hidden state and cell state of the i th layer at time step t , respectively.

The xLSTM architecture integrates sLSTM and mLSTM layers to proficiently capture both structured dependencies and multiplicative interactions, thereby offering a versatile framework that is optimized for modeling battery degradation.

The architectural design is predicated on the distinct attributes of battery degradation data. Initially, it is essential to ensure numerical stability when handling noisy voltage-current measurement data. Additionally, there is a necessity to accurately capture the intricate nonlinear coupling relationships among various degradation mechanisms. Moreover, it is imperative to sustain robust predictions across diverse charging strategies. In light of these requirements, a systematic evaluation of five configurations was conducted, encompassing single-layer baselines to multi-layer combinations.

As demonstrated in Table 1, a comparative analysis was performed among architectures characterized by differing layer compositions and depths. Single-layer configurations (s, m) served as baselines for evaluating the performance of individual components. The ‘s’ configuration exhibited satisfactory performance (MAPE = 0.28%), indicating the stability of sLSTM, while the ‘m’ configuration displayed elevated error rates (MAPE = 0.91%), suggesting a susceptibility to overfitting in mLSTM due to insufficient regularization. The two-layer ‘sm’ combination improved performance (MAPE=0.53%) by merging stability and complexity. However, the overall modeling capability remained inadequate.

The three-layer [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’] architecture demonstrated optimal performance, achieving a MAPE of 0.20% and an RMSE of 0.27%, thereby significantly surpassing other configurations. In contrast, the extension to a four-layer (’smsm’) architecture led to a decline in performance, as evidenced by a MAPE of 0.75%. This suggests the presence of diminishing returns and the potential for overfitting associated with increased model complexity.

The efficacy of the [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’] configuration is attributed to its functional specialization. The initial sLSTM layer is responsible for normalizing input sequences and addressing the inherent noise present in IC curve features, rendering it particularly adept at processing time-series battery data derived from various charging protocols. The intermediate mLSTM layer utilizes its matrix memory mechanism to capture intricate multiplicative interactions among electrochemical degradation factors. This capability is crucial for modeling the interactions of different degradation mechanisms, such as SEI growth, particle cracking, and lithium plating, under diverse charging strategies. The concluding sLSTM layer enhances prediction stability and mitigates the risk of overfitting through its normalizer state, thereby ensuring reliable SOH estimation across varying battery conditions.

This architectural selection achieves a balance between model expressiveness and generalization capability. Although more complex structures may be capable of capturing additional patterns within the training data, the results indicate that the three-layer design delivers optimal performance for practical battery health estimation across a range of operational conditions present in the dataset. The architecture of the proposed three-layer ([‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’]) xLSTM model is shown in Fig. 5.

To comprehensively assess the performance of the proposed SOH prediction model, this paper employs three widely used statistical metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Coefficient of Determination (
R
2
).
(26)
MAPE =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
|
SOH
i
−
SOH
̂
i
SOH
i
|
× 100 %
where n is the number of cycles,
SOH
i
is the actual value, and
SOH
̂
i
is the predicted value. A lower MAPE indicates higher prediction accuracy.

RMSE reflects the average deviation between predicted and actual values, calculated as:
(27)
RMSE =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
(
SOH
i
−
SOH
̂
i
)
2
RMSE is in the same unit as the dependent variable, with lower values indicating better model prediction performance.

R
2
measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variable(s). Its value ranges from 0 to 1, with values closer to 1 indicating better model fit. It is calculated as:
(28)
R
2
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
n
(
SOH
i
−
SOH
̂
i
)
2
∑
i = 1
n
(
SOH
i
−
SOH
¯
)
2
where
SOH
¯
is the mean of the actual SOH values.
These three metrics collectively provide a comprehensive evaluation of the model’s predictive performance, encompassing relative error, absolute error, and goodness of fit.

This section provides a thorough assessment of the proposed xLSTM based SOH estimation method, encompassing data partitioning strategies, model parameter configurations, and comprehensive performance comparisons with leading approaches. The validation includes ablation studies on feature selection strategies, robustness analysis in scenarios with incomplete data, cross-chemistry validation on NCM batteries, and architectural analysis that supports the optimal xLSTM configuration. This demonstrates the method’s efficacy across various operational conditions and battery chemistries.

This study utilizes two datasets for comprehensive validation. The primary dataset comes from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and Toyota Research Institute [28], containing 124 commercial high-performance lithium-ion batteries cycled to failure under fast-charging conditions with 72 different charging and discharging strategies. These lithium iron phosphate (LFP)/graphite batteries, manufactured by A123 Systems (APR18650M1 A), were cycled in a 48-channel Arbin LBT potentiostat at a forced convection temperature chamber set to 30 °C using horizontal cylindrical clamps. The nominal capacity of the batteries is 1.1 Ah, and the nominal voltage is 3.3 V. The fast-charging protocol employs a dual-stage constant-current (CC) strategy, denoted as “C1(Q1)-C2”. The first stage (C1) uses charging rates from 1C to 8C until reaching a specified state-of-charge percentage (Q1), followed by a second stage (C2) with rates between 3C and 6C. After 80% SOC, charging continues at 1C CC-CV. Upper and lower cutoff voltages are 3.6 V and 2.0 V respectively, meeting manufacturer specifications. During cycling, cells may hit the upper voltage limit, resulting in significant constant-voltage charging. All cells discharge at 4C. Fig. 6 shows the SOH versus cycle number for all batteries.

For cross-chemistry validation, the XJU NCM battery dataset was employed [29]. This dataset comprises 15 18650 nickel-cobalt-manganese batteries with a nominal capacity of 2000 mAh and nominal voltage of 3.6 V. The charging protocol involves 0.5C constant current-constant voltage charging for the first cycle, followed by 3.0C charging for subsequent cycles, with discharge rates of 0.2C and 1.0C respectively. All experiments were conducted at room temperature (approximately 20 °C).

To validate the accuracy and generalizability of the proposed method, batteries from both datasets were divided into training and testing sets. From the MIT LFP dataset, six batteries with varying charging strategies and cycle counts were selected as the test set to ensure comprehensive evaluation across different operating conditions. The specific information of the selected batteries is shown in Table 2. The remaining 118 batteries were used for training.

Similarly, from the XJU NCM dataset, six batteries were randomly selected as the test set, with the remaining 9 batteries serving as the training set. This cross-chemistry testing approach ensures that the proposed method is robust and applicable across different battery chemistries. The xLSTM model configuration used for both datasets is detailed in Table 3.

Data normalization is a crucial preprocessing step in training neural networks. It involves scaling the data to ensure that all input features contribute equally to the model’s learning process. One common method for normalization is the MinMaxScaler, which scales the data to a specific range, typically [0, 1]. The formula for MinMax scaling is given by:
(29)
X
scaled
=
X −
X
min
X
max
−
X
min
Where X is the original data,
X
min
is the minimum value of X , and
X
max
is the maximum value of X .

The xLSTM model is structured with an input dimension of 7, corresponding to the seven features identified during the feature extraction phase. Its architecture comprises three layers arranged as [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’], incorporating sLSTM layers for numerical stability, while the central mLSTM layer, with a dimension of
d = 256
, enhances temporal modeling through matrix memory mechanisms. The model employs two attention heads, each with a head size of 256, yielding a total hidden dimension of 512. Training occurs over 30 epochs with a batch size of 256, utilizing the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 and the MSE loss function. Both features and targets are normalized using MinMaxScaler to ensure stable convergence. All experiments are conducted on an NVIDIA RTX 4060 with 8 GB of VRAM. Detailed parameters are provided in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOH estimation performance for LFP batteries
ce:section.ce:para: The prediction results for the test batteries are presented in Fig. 7. The first column describes the predicted SOH and actual SOH at each charge–discharge cycle point. The images in the middle column represent the differences between the predicted SOH and actual SOH for each cycle. The rightmost column illustrates the errors between the predicted and true values for each cycle.

For example, through analysis of Fig. 7(a), the indicators in (27)–(29) mentioned above for battery b1c15 can be obtained as follows: MAPE = 0.22%, RMSE = 0.35%, and
R
2
= 0.995. These metrics indicate excellent prediction performance, with very low error rates and a near-perfect fit to the actual SOH values.

In Fig. 7(a), (b) and(c), it can be observed that despite these batteries having different charging strategies and cycle end points, the method proposed in this experiment can assess the health status relatively accurately. The error plots (right column of Fig. 7) for the six test batteries show that the prediction errors are consistently low, with the mean error for most batteries remaining within 1% over their entire cycle life.

To further validate the accuracy of the features extracted and methods applied in this study, the predictive indicators were compared with other approaches proposed in recent MIT dataset research for the test set batteries. For ensuring rigorous comparison, each baseline model was implemented exactly following their original parameter settings as described in their respective papers. For CNN-MMD [20], which uses maximum mean discrepancy domain adaptation technique for transfer learning-based SOH estimation, the specified network architecture and parameters were maintained. The REC-MCA-CNN [21], which integrates CNNs for feature extraction with Multi-scale Convolutional Attention mechanism, was implemented following their original configuration for capturing degradation features. For BiLSTM [22], which focuses on health indicator extraction from charge–discharge curves through battery aging mechanism analysis. The BiLSTM-Transformer [24], which combines BiLSTM for capturing long-term dependencies with transformer’s multi-head attention mechanism, was implemented according to their original specifications for fast charging conditions, Comparisons with conventional LSTM and GRU models are also incorporated as baseline references. The proposed xLSTM demonstrates a significant performance improvement over both models, with the average MAPE decreasing from 0.49% for LSTM and 0.37% for GRU to 0.20%, thereby illustrating the efficacy of the enhanced architecture. The findings of this extensive comparison are detailed in Table 4.

The results demonstrate that our proposed xLSTM model achieves superior performance across metrics and batteries. Specifically, our model achieves MAPE of approximately 0.2% for most batteries, which is significantly lower than the competing models. The RMSE maintains a consistently low error rate, generally below 0.3% across the test set. Furthermore,
R
2
exceeds 0.99 in all cases, indicating that our model explains over 99% of the variance in SOH data. The proposed xLSTM model achieves significant improvements across all evaluation metrics, reducing MAPE by 44.4% (from 0.36% to 0.20%) and RMSE by 41.3% (from 0.46% to 0.27%) compared to the best performing baseline method (CNN-MMD). Specifically, achieving an average MAPE of 0.20%, RMSE of 0.27%, and R
2
of 0.997, the model significantly outperforms CNN-MMD, REC-MCA-CNN, BiLSTM and BiLSTM-Transformer models through innovative deep learning techniques and advanced architectural design. This enhanced performance can be primarily attributed to three key aspects, a comprehensive feature extraction process, effective feature selection based on Spearman correlation coefficients, and an advanced xLSTM architecture capable of capturing complex temporal dependencies in battery degradation processes. Notably, the model maintains high accuracy across various battery types and charging strategies, demonstrating its robustness and generalization capability under diverse operational conditions, a crucial factor for practical industrial applications in battery health monitoring and management systems.

This subsection conducts comprehensive ablation studies to validate the design choices and assess the robustness of the proposed methodology. The analysis encompasses three critical aspects: feature selection methodology comparison, the impact of feature screening strategies, and model robustness under practical data constraints. These evaluations provide insights into the fundamental components contributing to the superior performance of the proposed xLSTM-based SOH estimation framework.

To validate the superiority of Spearman correlation-based feature selection, a comprehensive comparison was conducted with Kendall and Pearson correlation coefficient methods. All three methods employed the same threshold criterion, selecting features with absolute correlation coefficients greater than 0.8 as input variables. The correlation coefficients between each feature and SOH for different methods are presented in Table 5, where bold values indicate features exceeding the 0.8 threshold.

Based on the correlation analysis, the Kendall method identified five features (
S
2
,
S
7
,
S
10
,
S
11
,
S
12
) exceeding the 0.8 threshold, while the Pearson method selected four features (
S
7
,
S
10
,
S
11
,
S
12
). To systematically evaluate the effectiveness of different feature selection strategies, Table 6 presents the performance comparison using four approaches, all 13 features without selection, Pearson correlation-based selection, Kendall correlation-based selection, and the proposed method.

The results demonstrate significant performance variations across different feature selection strategies. When utilizing all 13 features without any selection criterion, the model achieved an average MAPE of 0.74%, RMSE of 0.81%, and
R
2
of 0.969, indicating substantial noise interference from irrelevant parameters. Pearson correlation-based selection showed marginal improvement with MAPE of 0.66% and RMSE of 0.79%, but the enhancement remained limited due to its assumption of linear relationships, which inadequately captures the complex nonlinear degradation patterns inherent in battery systems.

Kendall correlation-based selection exhibited notable improvement, achieving MAPE of 0.42% and RMSE of 0.49% with
R
2
of 0.989. This superior performance compared to Pearson correlation can be attributed to Kendall’s ability to capture monotonic relationships without strict linearity assumptions. However, the proposed Spearman correlation-based approach demonstrated the most substantial performance enhancement, achieving MAPE of 0.20%, RMSE of 0.27%, and
R
2
of 0.997. This represents a 73% improvement in MAPE compared to using all features and a 52% improvement over Kendall correlation.

The superior performance of Spearman correlation can be attributed to several key advantages in the context of battery SOH estimation. First, Spearman correlation’s non-parametric nature makes it particularly robust against outliers and measurement noise commonly present in electrochemical data. Second, its rank-based approach effectively captures monotonic relationships between IC curve features and SOH degradation without assuming specific functional forms. Third, the method’s insensitivity to data distribution characteristics ensures reliable feature selection across diverse charging strategies and battery conditions. These findings validate the strategic choice of Spearman correlation for feature selection in the proposed framework, confirming its ability to identify the most informative indicators while eliminating redundant parameters that could compromise model performance.

In practical scenarios, lithium-ion batteries may face circumstances where comprehensive charge–discharge cycle data is unattainable due to operational limitations or insufficient monitoring systems. The IC curve features analyzed in this study are derived from the initial discharge phase, which offers considerable advantages for real-world applications. During standard discharge operations, LFP batteries typically maintain a state-of-charge (SOC) above 60% until the voltage decreases to 3.1 V. The 60%–80% SOC range covers the main usage interval in most applications. By leveraging this principle, along with the five IC related features (
S
1
,
S
2
,
S
7
,
S
8
,
S
10
), effective SOH estimation can be conducted under conditions of fragmented data.

Table 7 illustrates the SOH estimation outcomes utilizing solely IC curve features in fragmented data scenarios. Despite relying exclusively on the extracted discharge IC curve characteristics, the model attains notable prediction accuracy, with average performance metrics of MAPE = 0.35%, RMSE = 0.43%, and
R
2
= 0.992. These findings indicate that electrochemical indicators derived from IC curves exhibit inherent robustness and can sustain reliable SOH estimation capabilities even in the absence of complete charging data.

The IC only approach, while exhibiting a performance trade-off relative to the complete feature set, evidenced by an increase in MAPE from 0.20% to 0.35%, still surpasses existing methodologies, thereby underscoring the efficacy of correlation-based feature selection. The consistently high
R
2
values, exceeding 0.99, suggest that IC curve features effectively encapsulate fundamental degradation mechanisms, rendering them advantageous for practical applications where data availability is constrained. The capacity to achieve reliable SOH estimation utilizing fragmented IC data addresses a significant challenge in real-world battery management systems, where continuous monitoring may be disrupted or incomplete charging profiles may be absent. This capability enhances the practical applicability of the proposed method across a range of operational scenarios, including electric vehicles with partial charging patterns and grid storage systems characterized by irregular charge–discharge cycles.

To assess the generalization capability across various battery chemistries, this study expands the evaluation to include NCM batteries sourced from the XJU dataset. These batteries possess a nominal capacity of 2000 mAh and a voltage of 3.6 V, exhibiting distinct electrochemical characteristics in comparison to the LFP batteries utilized in the primary evaluation. For NCM batteries, relevant features were analyzed, and the selected health factors also showed high correlation coefficients. For example, the correlation coefficients for battery #6 are shown in Fig. 8.

A selection of six NCM battery channels was randomly designated as the test set, employing the same seven features extracted through the established methodology. The results of the SOH estimation are presented in Table 8. The proposed xLSTM model demonstrates outstanding performance, achieving an average MAPE of 0.19%, an RMSE of 0.27%, and an
R
2
value of 0.996, which is notably consistent with the performance of LFP batteries (MAPE of 0.20%).

The comparable outcomes between the LFP and NCM datasets indicate that the extracted features, particularly the characteristics of the IC curve, effectively capture fundamental degradation mechanisms that are applicable across different types of lithium-ion batteries. This successful validation across chemistries affirms the general applicability of the proposed method and establishes it as a versatile solution for comprehensive battery health monitoring in a range of industrial applications.

This paper presents a novel xLSTM-based approach for robust SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries under diverse charging strategies. The proposed method integrates physics-informed feature extraction with advanced deep learning architectures to achieve superior prediction accuracy across various operational conditions.

The key contributions include the development of a systematic feature extraction process that leverages incremental capacity curve characteristics alongside voltage-current measurements, capturing fundamental electrochemical degradation mechanisms. The Spearman correlation-based feature selection effectively identifies the most relevant indicators while eliminating redundant parameters, enhancing both model performance and interpretability. The optimized three-layer xLSTM architecture [‘s’, ‘m’, ‘s’] combines sequence normalization, complex pattern recognition, and output stabilization to effectively model battery degradation processes. Comprehensive validation indicates outstanding performance, with an average MAPE of 0.20%, RMSE of 0.27%, and
R
2
of 0.997 across 124 commercial LFP batteries utilizing 72 distinct charging strategies. The proposed method significantly surpasses existing techniques, achieving over 40% enhancement in accuracy relative to state-of-the-art approaches. Cross-chemistry validation involving NCM batteries substantiates the method’s robust generalization capability, while ablation studies affirm the efficacy of correlation-based feature selection and the resilience of IC curve features in fragmented data scenarios.

The practical applicability is evidenced by successful SOH estimation utilizing partial discharge data, effectively addressing real-world limitations where comprehensive cycling information may be lacking. This capability, along with the method’s flexibility to accommodate various charging protocols, establishes it as a significant asset for industrial battery management systems across a range of applications, including electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage.

Future research avenues involve extending validation to extreme operating conditions (particularly varying temperature ranges and harsh environmental conditions), exploring transfer learning strategies for swift adaptation to new battery chemistries, developing solutions for genuinely fragmented data scenarios with random charge–discharge interruptions, and developing optimized implementations for real-time deployment in embedded battery management systems with detailed computational complexity analysis and memory footprint optimization for typical BMS microcontrollers.

Xiangjian Meng: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shixin Xu: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yongjin Yu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yanzheng Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Feng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
